name: Methylparaben
notes: Restricted in EU cosmetics (max 0.4% alone, 0.8% combined). Has weak estrogenic activity. Banned from certain leave-on body products in some markets.

name: Ethylparaben
notes: EU restricted paraben. Weak estrogenic activity. Allowed at max 0.4% alone or 0.8% combined in cosmetics.

name: Propylparaben
notes: EU restricted paraben. SCCS concluded it should not be used in cosmetics for children under 3 years. Shows estrogenic and potential reproductive toxicity.

name: Butylparaben
notes: EU restricted paraben. Stronger estrogenic activity than methyl/ethylparaben. Not recommended in products for children under 3. Banned from certain applications.

name: Isobutylparaben
notes: Banned in EU cosmetics as of 2014 (Regulation 358/2014). Strong estrogenic activity. Also banned in Denmark for children's products.

name: Isopropylparaben
notes: Banned in EU cosmetics as of 2014 (Regulation 358/2014). Strong estrogenic and endocrine disrupting properties.

name: Benzylparaben
notes: Banned in EU cosmetics as of 2014 (Regulation 358/2014). Significant estrogenic and endocrine disrupting activity.

name: Dibutyl phthalate
notes: Banned in EU cosmetics. CMR category 1B reproductive toxin and developmental toxin. SVHC under REACH. Disrupts hormones and development.

name: Diethylhexyl phthalate
notes: Banned in EU cosmetics. IARC Group 2B possible carcinogen. CMR substance. SVHC under REACH. Significant endocrine disrupting and reproductive toxicity properties.

name: Benzyl butyl phthalate
notes: Banned in EU cosmetics. CMR category 1B reproductive toxin. SVHC under REACH.

name: Diethyl phthalate
notes: Still permitted in some EU cosmetics as fixative. Some evidence of endocrine disrupting activity. Metabolite (MEP) detected in human urine widely. Subject of ongoing review.

name: Dimethyl phthalate
notes: Phthalate plasticiser and fixative. Less studied than DEHP/DBP but shares structural concerns. Regulatory review ongoing.

name: Diisobutyl phthalate
notes: Banned in EU cosmetics. CMR category 1B reproductive toxin. SVHC under REACH. Similar toxicity profile to DBP.

name: DMDM Hydantoin
notes: Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. Restricted in EU (max 0.6%). Formaldehyde is IARC Group 1 carcinogen. Can cause contact allergies and sensitisation.

name: Imidazolidinyl urea
notes: Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. Restricted in EU (max 0.6%). Common cause of contact allergic dermatitis. Releases formaldehyde slowly over time.

name: Diazolidinyl urea
notes: Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. Restricted in EU (max 0.5%). Releases more formaldehyde than imidazolidinyl urea. Significant sensitisation potential.

name: Quaternium-15
notes: One of the strongest formaldehyde-releasing preservatives. Restricted in EU (max 0.2%). High contact allergy rates; among the most common causes of cosmetic allergies.

name: Bronopol
notes: Broad-spectrum preservative. Releases formaldehyde and can form nitrosamines (potential carcinogens) in combination with amines. EU restricted (max 0.1%).

name: Sodium hydroxymethylglycinate
notes: Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. EU restricted (max 0.5%). SCCS noted potential for formaldehyde-type contact sensitisation.

name: Methenamine
notes: Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. EU restricted (max 0.15% in cosmetics). Releases formaldehyde in acidic conditions.

name: Benzophenone-3
notes: UV filter restricted in EU at max 6% (10% for full body sunscreens with label). SCCS noted endocrine disrupting potential. Causes coral reef bleaching. Banned in Hawaii for reef protection.

name: Benzophenone-4
notes: Water-soluble UV filter. EU restricted (max 10%). Some evidence of systemic absorption and endocrine disruption.

name: Homosalate
notes: UV filter. SCCS (2020) concluded not safe above 0.5%; EU has restricted to max 7.34%. Penetrates skin; metabolites show endocrine disrupting activity.

name: Octinoxate
notes: Most widely used UV-B filter. EU restricted (max 10%). Penetrates skin; estrogenic activity shown in animal studies. Harmful to coral reefs. Banned in Hawaii.

name: Octocrylene
notes: UV filter. EU restricted (max 10%). Degrades to benzophenone over time in products. Associated with photoallergic reactions and potential endocrine disruption.

name: 4-Methylbenzylidene camphor
notes: UV filter banned in EU (Annex II of Regulation 1223/2009). SCCS concluded it is an endocrine disruptor with thyroid and reproductive effects. Still used in some non-EU markets.

name: Sodium Lauryl Sulfate
notes: Strong anionic surfactant. Known skin and mucous membrane irritant. Damages skin barrier with prolonged exposure. Controversy around carcinogenicity unfounded; primary concern is irritation.

name: Sodium Laureth Sulfate
notes: Milder than SLS but can be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane (probable human carcinogen) as a manufacturing byproduct. Irritant at high concentrations.

name: Ammonium Lauryl Sulfate
notes: Anionic surfactant similar to SLS. Skin and mucous membrane irritant. Common in shampoos and cleansers.

name: Ammonium Laureth Sulfate
notes: Ethoxylated surfactant. Milder than ALS. Possible 1,4-dioxane contamination as manufacturing byproduct.

name: Cyclopentasiloxane
notes: Cyclic silicone. Banned in EU rinse-off cosmetics above 0.1% (Regulation 2018/1581). Persistent, bioaccumulative, and potentially toxic (PBT) substance. Endocrine disrupting concerns.

name: Cyclotetrasiloxane
notes: Cyclic silicone. Banned in EU rinse-off cosmetics above 0.1% (Regulation 2018/1581). Reproductive toxin category 2. PBT substance. More concerning than D5 due to reproductive toxicity.

name: Cyclomethicone
notes: Mixture of cyclic silicones (mainly D4 and D5). Subject to same EU restrictions as D4 and D5 in rinse-off products. PBT concerns.

name: Triclosan
notes: Antimicrobial agent. Banned in US OTC antiseptic products (FDA 2016). EU restricted to toothpaste (0.3%) and hand soap (0.3%). Thyroid disruption, antibiotic resistance, and environmental persistence concerns.

name: Triclocarban
notes: Antimicrobial agent banned in EU cosmetics. FDA banned from US OTC antiseptics (2016). Persistent in environment, bioaccumulates, potential endocrine disruptor.

name: BHA
notes: Antioxidant preservative. IARC Group 2B possible carcinogen. EU restricted in cosmetics. Potential endocrine disruptor. Banned in some countries for food use.

name: BHT
notes: Antioxidant preservative. Some evidence of carcinogenicity and endocrine disruption at high doses. Considered safe in cosmetics at approved levels by SCCS.

name: Hydroquinone
notes: Skin-lightening agent. Banned in EU OTC cosmetics (prescription only). IARC Group 3. Long-term use causes ochronosis (permanent skin darkening). Genotoxic in some in vitro studies.

name: Formaldehyde
notes: IARC Group 1 carcinogen. Restricted in EU cosmetics (max 0.1%). Must carry warning label above 0.05%. Many cosmetics contain formaldehyde-releasing preservatives instead.

name: Styrene
notes: IARC Group 2B possible carcinogen. Banned in EU cosmetics. Neurotoxic at high exposures.

name: Cocamide DEA
notes: IARC Group 2B possible carcinogen (animal data). Can react with nitrosamines. EU has restricted DEA compounds in cosmetics. California Prop 65 listed.

name: Lauramide DEA
notes: Foam booster and emulsifier. Risk of nitrosamine formation with other ingredients. EU restrictions on DEA compounds.

name: Triethanolamine
notes: pH adjuster and emulsifier. EU restricted (max 2.5% in face products). Can form carcinogenic nitrosamines with nitrosating agents. Restricted from leave-on facial products above certain concentrations.

name: Talc
notes: IARC Group 2B when used in perineal area. Possible asbestos contamination. EU restricts to grades free of asbestiform fibres. Major lawsuits (Johnson & Johnson) over ovarian cancer links.

name: Petrolatum
notes: EU restricts petrolatum to fully refined grades with no evidence of carcinogenicity. Mineral oil aromatic hydrocarbons (MOAH) are IARC Group 1 carcinogens if present as contaminants.

name: Mineral oil
notes: Must be fully refined per EU regulations. Untreated/mildly treated mineral oils are IARC Group 1 carcinogens. Fully refined cosmetic grade considered safe but review of MOAH contamination ongoing.

name: Parfum
notes: Catch-all term hiding complex mixtures. EU requires declaration of 26 listed fragrance allergens above 0.01% (leave-on) or 0.001% (rinse-off). New EU regulation (2023) expanding to 80+ allergens.

name: Aluminum chlorohydrate
notes: Antiperspirant active. SCCS (2020) concluded insufficient evidence to establish safety for damaged or shaved skin at current levels. Ongoing debate about breast cancer and Alzheimer's links; not conclusively established.

name: Aluminum zirconium tetrachlorohydrex
notes: Antiperspirant active. EU restricts use and requires aerosol products not to be inhaled. Same concerns as aluminum chlorohydrate; EU under review. Not approved for spray use on broken skin.

name: Nano titanium dioxide
notes: UV filter in nanofom. EU permits in rinse-off cosmetics and non-spray leave-on products. SCCS (2021) concluded unsafe when inhaled (e.g. spray sunscreens). Must be labelled [nano] in EU.

name: Nano zinc oxide
notes: UV filter in nanoform. EU permits in face cosmetics at max 25% but prohibits use in spray applications. SCCS noted cytotoxic potential. Must be labelled [nano] in EU.

name: PEG-100 Stearate
notes: PEG compound used as emulsifier. May be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane (probable carcinogen) and ethylene oxide as manufacturing byproducts. Acts as penetration enhancer, increasing absorption of other ingredients. Low-irritation profile when pure.

name: Retinoic acid
notes: Banned in EU OTC cosmetics (prescription only). Potent teratogen — can cause severe birth defects. Causes significant skin irritation. Only available as pharmaceutical product.

name: Retinyl palmitate
notes: Vitamin A ester. EU restricts retinol/retinyl esters in cosmetics (max 0.05% face, 0.3% body). NTP study raised photosensitivity and photocarcinogenicity concerns in sun-exposed skin. Avoid during pregnancy at high levels.

name: methylisothiazolinone
notes: SCCS/1521/13 (2013) concluded MIT is unsafe in leave-on cosmetics at any concentration. EU Commission Regulation 2016/1198 banned MIT from leave-on cosmetics; restricted in rinse-off at max 0.0015%. SCCS/1570/15 further concluded 0.0015% is not safe even in rinse-off for the general population, prompting ongoing regulatory review. One of the leading causes of epidemic cosmetic contact allergy in the 2010s. safety_level=avoid because the leave-on ban covers the majority of products (moisturisers, serums, shampoos left in, sunscreens); rinse-off use remains controversial.

name: methylchloroisothiazolinone
notes: SCCS/1155/08 (2009) and SCCS/1521/13 (2013). Typically used in the Kathon CG biocide blend (MCI:MIT 3:1 by mass, CAS 55965-84-9). EU Commission Regulation 2014/1121 banned MCI/MIT from leave-on cosmetics; restricted in rinse-off at max 0.0015% combined MCI+MIT. MCI is the more potent sensitiser of the two components: lower NOAEL than MIT, cross-reacts with MIT, and with thiuram rubber accelerants causing occupational cross-sensitisation. Must be declared on cosmetic labels by full INCI name.

name: p-phenylenediamine
notes: EU Annex III restricted substance (Regulation 1223/2009, entry 8): permitted in oxidative hair dye products at max 2.0% after mixing for use; prohibited in other cosmetics; product must carry warning 'Hair colorants can cause severe allergic reactions. Read and follow instructions. This product is not intended for use on persons under 16 years. Temporary black henna tattoos may increase the risk of allergy.' SCCS/1443/11. Most common cause of allergic contact dermatitis from hair dye globally; cross-reacts with IPPD (rubber), sulfonamides, PABA sunscreen agents, and azo dyes. IARC Group 3 (unclassifiable as to carcinogenicity).

name: polyethylene
notes: ECHA REACH Restriction Entry 78 (Commission Regulation (EU) 2023/2055): intentionally added microplastics (particles <5 mm) restricted in cosmetics effective October 2023 for rinse-off products; phase-out for leave-on products by October 2027. Polyethylene beads were widely used as exfoliants in scrubs and as texture-modifiers in make-up before the ban. Primary concern is environmental persistence and aquatic ecotoxicology — microplastics are not biodegradable, accumulate in marine food chains, and have been detected in human blood, placenta, and lung tissue. IARC Group 3 (polyethylene itself — no carcinogenicity classification). score_penalty=8 reflects environmental rather than direct toxicity concern.

name: nylon-12
notes: ECHA REACH Restriction Entry 78 (Regulation (EU) 2023/2055): polyamide microplastic particles restricted in cosmetics. Used as smooth-feel powder in loose powder foundations, setting powders, eyeshadow bases, and as exfoliant beads. Like other synthetic polymer microplastics, primary concern is environmental persistence — not biodegradable, contributes to marine and terrestrial microplastic pollution. Nylon-12 particles have been detected in environmental samples globally. Phase-out timeline: rinse-off cosmetics October 2023; leave-on cosmetics October 2027. score_penalty=8 reflects environmental rather than direct human toxicity concern at typical cosmetic use levels.
